reference: F. Tebbenjohanns, M. Frimmer, and L. Novotny, "Optimal position detection of a dipolar scatterer in a focused field," Phys. Rev. A 100, 043821 (2019).
reported_imprecision: Heisenberg-limited: imprecision-backaction product satisfies uncertainty relation with equality (Eqs. 8 x Eqs. 4)
reported_backaction: total axial backaction along z given by Eqs. (4); with imprecision Eqs. (8) fills Heisenberg relation with equality
notes: Ideal optimal measurement scheme (full 4*pi weighted collection). '>90% of the entire information about the position along the z axis is contained in the field scattered in the backward direction (half-space z<0)' -- recorded as reported_detection_efficiency=0.9 (a lower bound / >90% statement, not an exact value). Point-dipole (Rayleigh) limit; distance to focus << wavelength. Primary theory anchor. e^{-iomega t} convention.
provenance: Main text Sec. II C, p.4 ('more than 90% ... in the backward direction (half-space z<0)'); Fig. 2(c,e,f); Eqs. (4),(7),(8).

reference: F. Tebbenjohanns, M. Frimmer, and L. Novotny, "Optimal position detection of a dipolar scatterer in a focused field," Phys. Rev. A 100, 043821 (2019).
reported_imprecision: eta_bw_z relative to Heisenberg limit (ratio S_z,Heisenberg / S_z,bw)
notes: Realistic backscatter (quadrant/split) detection with the trapping objective as collection lens; reference field = truncated plane wave, pi/2 phase-locked. eta_bw_z = 0.6 at NA_tl = 0.8, reaching >0.6 for realistic trapping lenses NA_tl > 0.8. Abstract states 'more than 60% of the Heisenberg limit' for the axial mode. Transverse eta_bw_x = eta_fw_x, eta_bw_y = eta_fw_y. Dipole/Rayleigh limit. This is the G-LIMIT dipole gate case (VALIDATION.md).
provenance: Fig. 3(c) and main text Sec. III B, p.5-6 ('efficiency to be as high as eta_bw_z = 0.6' for NA_tl = 0.8; caption 'exceeding 0.6 for realistic trapping lenses with NA_tl > 0.8'); Abstract.

reference: F. Tebbenjohanns, M. Frimmer, and L. Novotny, "Optimal position detection of a dipolar scatterer in a focused field," Phys. Rev. A 100, 043821 (2019).
notes: Forward four-quadrant split detection, NA_tl = 0.85 trapping lens. At NA_cl = 0.7: transverse eta_fw_x ~ eta_fw_y ~ 0.1, whereas longitudinal eta_fw_z ~ 0.001 (about two orders of magnitude smaller; eta_fw_z vanishes for a symmetric NA_cl = NA_tl setup). Reported_detection_efficiency here is the transverse-x value ~0.1 (approximate, read from text). Dipole/Rayleigh limit.
provenance: Fig. 3(b) and main text Sec. III A, p.5 ('eta_fw_x ~ eta_fw_y ~ 0.1, while for the longitudinal mode it is about two orders of magnitude smaller (eta_fw_z ~ 0.001)').

reference: P. Maurer, C. Gonzalez-Ballestero, and O. Romero-Isart, "Quantum theory of light interaction with a Lorenz-Mie particle: Optical detection and three-dimensional ground-state cooling," Phys. Rev. A 108, 033714 (2023); Erratum Phys. Rev. A 109, 019901 (2024).
reported_backaction: recoil heating rate Gamma_mu (Fig. 3); detection efficiency eta^d_mu = integral of information radiation pattern over collection solid angle, Eq. (24)
notes: Small-particle (Rayleigh, R/lambda0 -> 0) limit, high-NA running-wave config: eta^d_z = 0.55 for NA_L = 0.75 backward detection, well above the 1/9 ~ 0.11 ground-state-cooling threshold. Refractive index from relative permittivity epsilon = 2.07 (Table I) -> n = sqrt(2.07) = 1.4387 (silica, non-absorbing). lambda0 = 1550 nm, P = 200 mW, filling factor f0 = 10, waist overfills lens. Consistent with Tebbenjohanns2019 backward-z. e^{-iomega t} convention (Eq. before Eq.(27)). Detection efficiency defined per Eq.(24).
provenance: Fig. 4(a) first column and main text Sec. IV, p.9 ('large detection efficiency eta^d_z = 0.55 >> 1/9 for the high-NA configuration'); Eq. (24) definition; Table I parameters.

reference: P. Maurer, C. Gonzalez-Ballestero, and O. Romero-Isart, "Quantum theory of light interaction with a Lorenz-Mie particle: Optical detection and three-dimensional ground-state cooling," Phys. Rev. A 108, 033714 (2023); Erratum Phys. Rev. A 109, 019901 (2024).
reported_backaction: large recoil localization parameter accompanies large back-scattered fraction (high reflectance in Fabry-Perot regime)
notes: Mie regime, R/lambda0 = 2.24 (and 2.33) at NA_L = 0.75 running-wave backward detection: 'very high detection efficiencies of up to eta^d_z >~ 0.9'. sphere_radius_m = 2.24 * 1550 nm = 3.472e-6 m computed from R/lambda0 = 2.24. Reported value 0.9 is a '>~' (approximately >=) statement read from text, not an exact tabulated number. Refractive index n=1.4387 from epsilon=2.07 (Table I). Mie-regime efficiency data point.
provenance: Fig. 4(a) and main text Sec. IV, p.10 ('For the z-axes this leads to very high detection efficiencies of up to eta^d_z >~ 0.9'); local min/max at R/lambda0 = 2.24, 2.33 (Fig. 3).

reference: P. Maurer, C. Gonzalez-Ballestero, and O. Romero-Isart, "Quantum theory of light interaction with a Lorenz-Mie particle: Optical detection and three-dimensional ground-state cooling," Phys. Rev. A 108, 033714 (2023); Erratum Phys. Rev. A 109, 019901 (2024).
notes: Necessary condition for center-of-mass ground-state cooling along an axis (n-bar < 1) is total efficiency eta_mu > 1/9 ~= 0.111 (Eq. 23-24). reported_detection_efficiency records this threshold value 1/9 (a theoretical bound, not a measured efficiency). Included as the definition/threshold context record for the Mie-regime benchmark family.
provenance: Eqs. (23)-(25), main text Sec. III, p.6 ('a necessary condition to achieve center-of-mass ground-state cooling ... is eta_mu > 1/9 ~ 0.11').

reference: L. Magrini, P. Rosenzweig, C. Bach, A. Deutschmann-Olek, S. G. Hofer, S. Hong, N. Kiesel, A. Kugi, and M. Aspelmeyer, "Real-time optimal quantum control of mechanical motion at room temperature," Nature 595, 373-377 (2021).
reported_imprecision: imprecision-backaction product hbar/sqrt(eta) = 1.7 hbar (< factor 2 from Heisenberg limit); position uncertainty 1.3 x zero-point fluctuation
reported_backaction: backaction decoherence Gamma_ba = 2*pi*18.8 kHz; gas Gamma_th = 2*pi*0.6 kHz; quantum cooperativity C_q = 30
notes: Optical tweezer NA=0.95, lambda0=1064 nm, ~300 mW, x-polarized; silica 71.5 nm radius (~2.8e-18 kg). Confocal forward position sensing along optical axis z. Information collection efficiency eta = Gamma_meas/(Gamma_ba + Gamma_th) = 0.34. Ground-state feedback cooling to n = 0.56 +/- 0.02. Refractive index not stated in paper -> null (standard fused silica m ~ 1.45 at 1064 nm not asserted by source). Experimental efficiency record.
provenance: Main text p.2-3 ('information collection efficiency eta = Gamma_meas/(Gamma_ba+Gamma_th) = 0.34'; 'imprecision-backaction product of hbar/sqrt(eta) = 1.7 hbar'); setup params ('NA = 0.95, lambda0 = 1064 nm, ... 71.5 nm radius').

reference: F. Tebbenjohanns, M. L. Mattana, M. Rossi, M. Frimmer, and L. Novotny, "Quantum control of a nanoparticle optically levitated in cryogenic free space," Nature 595, 378-382 (2021).
reported_imprecision: measurement efficiency eta_meas = Gamma_meas/Gamma_tot = 0.24 +/- 0.02 (bounded by Heisenberg measurement-disturbance relation)
notes: Cryogenic free-space single-beam trap; NA=0.75 aspheric trapping lens; lambda=1550 nm, 1.2 W; silica nominal diameter 100 nm (measured 106 +/- 5 nm => radius ~53 nm). Backscattered field via optical circulator, homodyne + heterodyne detectors, z (optical axis) motion. Measurement efficiency eta_meas = Gamma_meas/Gamma_tot = 0.24 +/- 0.02; ground-state cooling to n-bar = 0.66 +/- 0.08. Refractive index not stated -> null. Realizes the Tebbenjohanns2019 backscatter scheme experimentally.
provenance: Main text p.3 ('measurement efficiency eta_meas = Gamma_meas/Gamma_tot = 0.24 +/- 0.02'); Fig. 1 caption and Methods (NA 0.75, lambda 1550 nm, diameter 100 nm, measured 106+/-5 nm, backscatter homodyne/heterodyne).

reference: U. Delic, M. Reisenbauer, K. Dare, D. Grass, V. Vuletic, N. Kiesel, and M. Aspelmeyer, "Cooling of a levitated nanoparticle to the motional quantum ground state," Science 367, 892-895 (2020).
notes: Cavity coherent-scattering ground-state cooling. Silica nominal diameter 143 +/- 4 nm (radius ~71.5 nm); tweezer lambda=1064 nm, waist Wx~0.67 um, Wy~0.77 um, P~400 mW; microscope objective NA=0.8. Cooled cavity-axis (x) motion to n_x = 0.43 +/- 0.03 phonons. collection_direction='cavity' (coherent scattering into cavity mode), not free-space homodyne; no free-space detection efficiency reported -> null. Included for parameter breadth (cavity scheme). Refractive index not stated -> null.
provenance: Abstract and main text p.1-2 ('silica sphere of 143 nm diameter ... n_x = 0.43 +/- 0.03 phonons'); Fig. 1 caption (lambda 1064 nm, Wx 0.67 um, Wy 0.77 um, P 400 mW, MO NA=0.8).

reference: D. Windey, C. Gonzalez-Ballestero, P. Maurer, L. Novotny, O. Romero-Isart, and R. Reimann, "Cavity-based 3D cooling of a levitated nanoparticle via coherent scattering," Phys. Rev. Lett. 122, 123601 (2019).
notes: Cavity-based 3D cooling via coherent scattering; tweezer light scattered into a blue-detuned cavity mode. Silica nominal diameter 136 nm (radius ~68 nm); lambda=1550.0(5) nm, trapping objective NA=0.83. Cooling of all three translational DOF (cavity axis to mK, transverse to few-hundred mK). collection_direction='cavity'; no free-space position detection efficiency reported -> null. Refractive index not stated -> null. Parameter-breadth record (cavity coherent scattering).
provenance: Fig. 1 and main text ('wavelength lambda = 1550.0(5) nm ... numerical aperture NA = 0.83 ... particle with 136 nm nominal diameter').

reference: L. Dania, K. Heidegger, D. S. Bykov, G. Cerchiari, G. Araneda, and T. E. Northup, "Position measurement of a levitated nanoparticle via interference with its mirror image," Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 013601 (2022).
notes: Self-homodyne detection via interference of the scattered light with its mirror image (a retro-reflecting mirror), charged silica nanoparticle in a Paul trap in high vacuum. Silica sphere 300 nm diameter (radius ~150 nm). Self-homodyne laser 780 nm, auxiliary forward-detection laser 987 nm; aspheric lenses NA=0.18. Reported detection efficiency 2.1% (=0.021) at NA=0.18, compared against forward detection. wavelength_m=780 nm (the self-homodyne detection laser). Refractive index not stated -> null. Experimental efficiency record; low-NA self-homodyne. Snowball: same Northup group as Cerchiari2021 self-homodyne theory.
provenance: Abstract ('detection efficiency of 2.1%, with a numerical aperture of 0.18'; sensitivity 1.7e-12 m/sqrt(Hz)); setup ('silica sphere 300 nm in diameter'; self-homodyne 780 nm, forward 987 nm laser, aspheric NA=0.18).

reference: G. Cerchiari, L. Dania, D. S. Bykov, R. Blatt, and T. E. Northup, "Position measurement of a dipolar scatterer via self-homodyne detection," Phys. Rev. A 104, 053523 (2021).
reported_imprecision: S_min = 5*hbar*c*lambda/(32*pi^2*P) (best case, spherical-mirror self-homodyne, linear-polarized dipole); S_max = 5*hbar*c*lambda/(16*pi^2*P)
notes: Theory of self-homodyne position detection using a concentric spherical mirror that images the dipole onto itself. For linearly polarized dipoles the lowest achievable imprecision is S_min = 5 hbar c lambda/(32 pi^2 P); the ratio of total imprecision in the spherical-mirror scheme vs the ideal configuration is only ~1/2. Point-dipole (Rayleigh) theory; parameters symbolic (P = collected power, lambda = wavelength) -> radius/NA/wavelength null. Imprecision recorded symbolically as reported per source.
provenance: Main text Sec. III-IV ('lowest imprecision S_min = 5 hbar c lambda/(32 pi^2 P) ... S_max = 5 hbar c lambda/(16 pi^2 P)'; imprecision ratio ~1/2 vs ideal).

reference: M. Kamba, R. Shimizu, and K. Aikawa, "Nanoscale feedback control of six degrees of freedom of a near-sphere," Nat. Commun. 14, 7943 (2023).
notes: Six-DOF feedback control of a near-spherical silica nanoparticle in an optical lattice; average radius 174(3) nm. Trap laser lambda=1550 nm, focused with NA=0.85 objective, ~176 mW per beam. Optical-lattice (standing-wave) trap; translational z motion cooled to n_z = 0.69(18) via optical feedback; librational (angular) DOF also cooled. No single position detection efficiency reported -> null. Refractive index not stated -> null. Parameter-breadth record (standing-wave lattice, angular DOF).
provenance: Main text p.1-2 ('average radius of 174(3) nm ... n_z = 0.69(18)'); Methods ('wavelength of 1550 nm ... objective lens (NA=0.85) ... power of 176 mW').

reference: L. Dania, D. S. Bykov, F. Goschin, M. Teller, A. Kassid, and T. E. Northup, "Ultrahigh quality factor of a levitated nanomechanical oscillator," Phys. Rev. Lett. 132, 133602 (2024).
notes: Silica nanoparticle in a linear Paul trap at room temperature, pressures as low as 7e-11 mbar; quality factor Q > 1e10 (dissipation-focused, not a detection-efficiency paper). Nominal diameter 300 nm (radius ~150 nm), charge q=+300(30)e; 780 nm detection laser, 21 mW. Trap is a Paul trap (not optical); detection NA not stated -> null. No detection efficiency reported -> null. Included for parameter breadth (Paul-trap, 780 nm detection).
provenance: Abstract ('silica nanoparticle in a linear Paul trap ... 7e-11 mbar ... Q > 1e10'); main text ('nominal diameter of 300 nm ... 780 nm laser beam with a power of 21 mW').

reference: L. Wang, L.-M. Zhou, Y. Tian, L.-H. Liu, G.-C. Guo, Y. Zheng, and F.-W. Sun, "Optimal position detection of an optically levitated Mie particle," arXiv:2408.15483 (2024).
notes: Theory of optimal position detection of a Mie particle. Backward detection, incident Gaussian beam NA_in=0.9. Mie regime R/lambda0 >~ 0.5 gives high information collection efficiency eta_c ~ 0.9 for specific size parameters; efficiency oscillates periodically in R/lambda0 with period ~1/(4n). sphere_radius_m = 2.0 * 1064 nm = 2.128e-6 (R/lambda0 = 2, the value used for the field/mode-matching figures). Silica n=1.44 (Table I), lambda0=1064 nm, P_in=0.1 W, waist w0=2.25 mm, focal length 2 mm. To-verify anchor family (Mie-regime); numbers read from Fig. 2(c) and text.
provenance: Fig. 2(c) and main text Sec. II ('information collection efficiency eta_c ~ 0.9 for Mie particles with specific size parameters R/lambda0'); Table I (n=1.44, lambda0=1064 nm, NA=0.9).

reference: S. Laing, S. Klomp, G. Winstone, A. Grinin, A. Dana, Z. Wang, K. S. Widyatmodjo, J. Bateman, and A. A. Geraci, "Optimal displacement detection of arbitrarily-shaped levitated dielectric objects using optical radiation," arXiv:2409.00782 (2024).
notes: Generalizes information-radiation-pattern / detection-efficiency theory to arbitrarily-shaped dielectric objects (SCUFF-EM BEM). Validated against the sphere results of Tebbenjohanns2019 / Maurer2023 (Figs. 3-4 reproduce sphere IRPs and detection efficiencies eta_mu for NA=0.1 and NA=0.75 collection). lambda=1550 nm. Per-radius detection-efficiency numbers appear only inside figure panels (not machine-readable from the PDF text) -> reported_detection_efficiency null pending figure digitization. To-verify anchor (LITERATURE.md sec 2 recent list); confirmed accessible. Snowball: cited by / cites the Tebbenjohanns2019 anchor.
provenance: Abstract and Sec. IV-V ('reproduce the results for spheres ... as given in [Tebbenjohanns/Maurer]'; Figs. 3-4 detection efficiencies eta_mu for NA=0.1 focusing and NA=0.75 collection; lambda=1550 nm).

reference: C. M. Pluchar, W. He, J. Manley, N. Deshler, S. Guha, and D. J. Wilson, "Imaging-based quantum optomechanics," Phys. Rev. Lett. 135, 023601 (2025).
reported_imprecision: imprecision-backaction product derived to equal the single-mode SQL in the purely-spatial (intermodal) coupling limit (Eq. 3); backaction from spatiotemporal photon shot noise
reported_backaction: spatiotemporal (intermodal) photon shot noise; SQL-saturating in the optimal-receiver limit
notes: Imaging-based (spatial-mode-sorter) optomechanics of a membrane flexural mode -- NOT a levitated sphere; recorded for detection-scheme breadth (imaging) and the imprecision-backaction analysis. Gaussian probe HG0 mode diameter 2*w0 = 0.6 um. Photodetection efficiency eta appears symbolically (receiver ideality), no numeric detection efficiency for a levitated particle -> null. To-verify anchor (Pluchar PRL 135 023601 2025); confirmed. NOT promoted to a benchmark (not a Lorenz-Mie sphere).
provenance: Abstract and Sec. II-IV ('imaging of the flexural modes of a membrane ... imprecision-backaction product ... equivalent to the SQL for single-mode optomechanics', Eq. 3); Fig. 1 (HG mode diameter 2w0=0.6 um).
